coredata.dc:title: A novel collapse strategy of zeolitic imidazole frameworks shell triggered by p-benzoquinone for the fluorescence monitoring α-glucosidase activity and screening natural anti-diabetes drug
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Wei
Lu, Yuexiang
Yao, Nan
Zhang, Xiwen
Wang, Nan
Liu, Yueying
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent materials encapsulated into zeolite imidazole framework-8 (ZIF-8) as fluorescent probes are widely applied to detect the various analytes. Notably, these analytes react with Zn2+ through the coordination, oxidation, or acidification of 2-methylimidazole (2-MIM) to cause the degradation of ZIF-8 shell, which enormously constraints the improvement of the sensor sensitivity and selectivity. Herein, we have rationally designed the new way to collapse the shell of ZIF-8 entrapped with copper nanoclusters (CuNCs@ZIF-8) triggered by p-benzoquinone. It is worth noting that p-benzoquinone reactions with 2-MIM through hydrogen bonding interaction. As a proof-of-concept, a sensitive method for the detection of hydroquinone (HQ) is developed based on the degradation of CuNCs@ZIF-8 because HQ is easily oxidized to p-benzoquinone. The limit of detection (LOD) is estimated as low as 76 nM. Importantly, this method is highly selective for HQ over other dihydroxybenzene isomers. Inspired by the generation of HQ from hydrolysis of α-arbutin as a substrate for α-glucosidase (α-Glu), we have further proposed a straightforward α-Glu activity assay. LOD is down to 0.003 U/mL, which is lower than most of other α-Glu activity assays. Moreover, the inhibition effect of acarbose and three kinds of natural flavonoids including baicalein, silibinin, and apigenin on α-Glu activity is measured with IC50 values of 37, 155, 1940, and 2930 nM, respectively. The synergistic and antagonistic inhibition effect of α-Glu activity is also evaluated by binary combination with three types of flavonoids. This approach provides a promising strategy in clinical diagnosis and anti-diabetic drug screening.

CuNCs@ZIF-8 characterization: (D) Particle size distribution by DLS, (E) FTIR spectra, (F) XRD, (G) Mapping Elemental mapping images, (H) XPS spectra, (I) High-resolution XPS spectra for Cu 2p, and (J) Normalized fluorescence excitation (blue) and emission (red) spectra of CuNCs (dash line) and CuNCs@ZIF-8 (solid line).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of (1) CuNCs (black curve), (2) CuNCs@ZIF-8 +HQ (blue curve), and (3) CuNCs@ZIF-8 (red curve). Insert: corresponding photo images under 365 nm UV light radiation. (B) TEM image of CuNCs@ZIF-8 + HQ. (C) Particles size distribution of CuNCs@ZIF-8 before and after adding HQ. (D) Fluorescent lifetime decay for CuNCs, CuNCs@ZIF-8 and CuNCs@ZIF-8 +HQ. (E) Fluorescence excitation spectra of CuNCs@ZIF-8 (1), fluorescence emission spectra of CuNCs@ZIF-8 (2), and UV–vis absorption spectra of HQ (3). (F) UV–vis absorption spectra: (1) HQ, (2) 2-MIM, and (3) HQ+ 2-MIM. (G) FTIR spectra of (1) HQ, (2) 2-MIM, and (3) HQ+ 2-MIM. (H) Fluorescence emission spectra of CuNCs@ZIF-8, CuNCs@ZIF-8 +GSH, CuNCs@ZIF-8 +HQ, and CuNCs@ZIF-8 +HQ+GSH. (I) Zeta potential of CuNCs@ZIF-8, and CuNCs@ZIF-8 +HQ.

(C) Relationship between the fluorescence intensity at 600 nm and the HQ concentrations. (D) Calibration plots between fluorescence responses and HQ concentrations. (E) Selectivity of CuNCs@ZIF-8 based sensor against 60 µM of analogs and other various compounds. (F) UV–vis absorption spectra of 50 µM of HQ (a), CC (b), and RS (c) with 2-MIM.

(A) Illustration of CuNCs@ZIF-8 and α-arbutin for α-Glu assay and its inhibitors screening base on CuNCs@ZIF-8. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra: (1) CuNCs@ZIF-8, (2) CuNCs@ZIF-8 + α-arbutin, (3) CuNCs@ZIF-8 + α-Glu, (4) CuNCs@ZIF-8 +HQ, and (5) CuNCs@ZIF-8 + α-arbutin+ α-Glu. (C) Size distribution of hydrodynamic diameter for CuNCs@ZIF-8 + α-arbutin+ α-Glu by DLS. Inset: TEM images. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of CuNCs@ZIF-8 and α-arbutin upon the addition of α-Glu with different concentrations. (E) F versus α-Glu with different concentrations. Inset: calibration plots between I and α-Glu concentrations. (F) Selectivity of CuNCs@ZIF-8 based sensing platform for α-Glu activity detection. The concentration of each metal ions, amino acids, protein, and enzyme was 1.0 mM, 1.0 mM, 0.05 mg /mL, 0.5 U /mL, respectively.

The fluorescent fitting curve of inhibition efficiency versus concentration of (C) baicalein, (D) silibinin, and (E) apigenin. (F) Inhibitory effects of different kinds and concentrations of inhibitors on α-Glu by fluorescence detection mode.

head.ce:title: A novel collapse strategy of zeolitic imidazole frameworks shell triggered by p-benzoquinone for the fluorescence monitoring α-glucosidase activity and screening natural anti-diabetes drug

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent materials encapsulated into zeolite imidazole framework-8 (ZIF-8) as fluorescent probes are widely applied to detect the various analytes. Notably, these analytes react with Zn2+ through the coordination, oxidation, or acidification of 2-methylimidazole (2-MIM) to cause the degradation of ZIF-8 shell, which enormously constraints the improvement of the sensor sensitivity and selectivity. Herein, we have rationally designed the new way to collapse the shell of ZIF-8 entrapped with copper nanoclusters (CuNCs@ZIF-8) triggered by p-benzoquinone. It is worth noting that p-benzoquinone reactions with 2-MIM through hydrogen bonding interaction. As a proof-of-concept, a sensitive method for the detection of hydroquinone (HQ) is developed based on the degradation of CuNCs@ZIF-8 because HQ is easily oxidized to p-benzoquinone. The limit of detection (LOD) is estimated as low as 76 nM. Importantly, this method is highly selective for HQ over other dihydroxybenzene isomers. Inspired by the generation of HQ from hydrolysis of α-arbutin as a substrate for α-glucosidase (α-Glu), we have further proposed a straightforward α-Glu activity assay. LOD is down to 0.003 U/mL, which is lower than most of other α-Glu activity assays. Moreover, the inhibition effect of acarbose and three kinds of natural flavonoids including baicalein, silibinin, and apigenin on α-Glu activity is measured with IC50 values of 37, 155, 1940, and 2930 nM, respectively. The synergistic and antagonistic inhibition effect of α-Glu activity is also evaluated by binary combination with three types of flavonoids. This approach provides a promising strategy in clinical diagnosis and anti-diabetic drug screening.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A novel strategy has been developed to decompose CuNCs@ZIF-8 shell for the detection of hydroquinone and α-glucosidase activity. Furthermore, this sensing system has not only been applied to the determination of IC50 value for various inhibitors but also binary combined effect of these inhibitors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: 1,4-dihydroxybenzene or hydroquinone (HQ), as a kind of phenolic compound, has been extensively applied in dyes, antioxidants, pharmaceuticals, pesticides, plastics, and other industrial fields [1]. Owing to its high toxicity and low degradability, it has been included in the lists of priority pollutants to be monitored in the aquatic environment by both of U.S. EPA and European Union (EU) [2]. Unfortunately, the high level of HQ may lead to many diseases, such as skin inflammation, headache, edema, collapse, and nausea [3]. Currently, various methods have been developed for hydroquinone detection, including gas chromatography/high-performance liquid chromatography [4,5], electrochemical method [6], chemiluminescence [7], colorimetric assay [8,9], and so on. Although they can achieve good performance, most of these methods are usually plagued by expensive instruments, complex operation, and time-consuming, or the need for electrode modification, or complex nanozymes synthesis for colorimetric assay. In comparison, fluorescence method is favored by researchers for its advantages of high sensitivity, rapid responses, and simple operation. Various materials including semiconductor quantum dots [10], carbon-based quantum dot [11,12], conjugated polymer [13], silicon quantum dots [2,14], and silver nanocluster [15] as fluorescence probes are designed for the detection of HQ. Their sensing mechanism mainly depends on the quenching effect of HQ or p-benzoquinone on the fluorescence probe through electron transfer or inner filter effect (IFE). In addition, catechol (CC) and resorcinol (RS) usually have huge interference on the HQ detection as their physical and chemical properties are similar. Above all, it is quite imperative to develop a new sensing strategy for the highly sensitive and selective detection of HQ, especially distinguishing HQ among the coexisting dihydroxybenzene isomers.

Besides being considered as a target analyte, HQ also plays the role of messenger in a variety of enzyme analysis systems, which provides the foundation for enzyme detection [16,17]. For example, under the catalysis of α-Glu, α-arbutin or 4-hydroxyphenyl-α-D-glucopyranosyl (4-HPG) as substrates can be hydrolyzed to release HQ [16–18], which not only meets the requirement of developing a method to detect HQ, but also opens a way for the analysis of α-Glu activity. α-Glu, a membrane binding enzyme in the small intestine, plays an important role in carbohydrate digestion by specifically hydrolyzing α-glucopyranyl to produce glucose [19]. However, excessive levels of α-Glu in the body can lead to chronic injury and dysfunction. α-Glu inhibitors can effectively reduce the activity of α-Glu and thus delay the absorption of carbohydrate in the gut, so they are considered as one of the effective anti-diabetic drugs [16]. Compared with synthetic drugs, natural products as α-Glu inhibitors have less side effects, lower cost, and higher hypoglycemic efficacy [19]. To the best of our knowledge, most of the fluorescence methods for monitoring α-Glu activity employ L-ascorbic acid-2-O-α-D-glucopyranosyl (AAG), and p-nitrophenyl-α-D-glucopyranoside (PNGP) as enzyme substrates. It is worth noting that α-Glu can catalytically hydrolyze AAG to produce ascorbic acid (AA). The available AAG substrates have been extensively designed for α-Glu activity assay and its inhibitors screening mainly based on redox-induced fluorescent sensing strategy, which is attributed to the oxidation reaction between AA and some oxidant materials including metal ions (Fe3+ [20] and Ce4+ [21]), small molecules (H2O2) [22], and nanomaterials (MnO2 nanosheet [23–26], V2O5 nanoribbons [27], hexagonal CoOOH nanosheet [28]). An alternative PNGP substrate or its hydrolysis product for the detection of α-Glu activity and screening its inhibitors is developed mostly by using IFE [19,29–32]. Although there are rarely reports on the α-Glu activity analysis and screening its inhibitors based on HQ-triggered fluorescent reactions from 4-HPG or α-arbutin substrate [16,17]. Their sensitivity still needs to be further improved. Therefore, the development of efficient methods for the detection of α-Glu activity and screening natural α-Glu inhibitors is very meaningful for the clinical treatment of diabetes.

ZIF-8 composed of Zn2+ ions and 2-methyl imidazole (2-MIM) with three-dimensional porous crystalline structure become an excellent candidate to embed ﬂuorescent species for the detection of various analytes due to their mild preparation conditions, large specific surface area, and high encapsulated efficiency. Notably, the entrapment of luminescent materials including metal nanoclusters [33–37], organic dyes [38–41], semiconductor/carbon/sulfur quantum dots [42–45] into ZIF-8 is one of the most efficient strategies to simultaneously improve the fluorescent emission and stability of luminescent species due to the confinement and protection of hierarchical pore structure of ZIF-8. Furthermore, the porous structure helps the enrichment of analyte from the complex matrix, increasing the sensitivity and selectivity of analytical methods [43–45]. Notably, these sensors mainly depend on the release of luminescent materials after the addition of response stimulus through breakage of the coordination bonds between zinc and 2-MIM, which results in fluorescence signal changes. The collapse of ZIF-8 shell can be mainly controlled by three pathways. Firstly, ZIF-8 skeleton can be destroyed by lowering the pH below 6.5. Some studies unveil that the mechanism of ZIF-8 disassemble attributes to insertion of a water or acidic molecule into the Zn-N bond, follows by the subsequent dissociation of the protonated imidazole ligand away from the framework structure [46]. The crystallographic facet of ZIF-8 contains the large six-member rings, which is more susceptible to protonation over its counterpart with the smaller four-member rings [47]. Secondly, ZIF-8 scaffold can be decomposed after adding some agents to chelate with Zn2+ (e.g. EDTA and phosphate) [48]. Finally, the structure of ZIF-8 can be degraded due to the redox reaction between 2-MIM with oxidants such as H2O2 [33,49]. Noticeably, the a quinone-imine bond is formed between p-benzoquinone and imines groups of polyethyleneimines [16]. So, we speculate that phenols or quinones can degrade ZIF-8 shell by simultaneous reacting with 2-MIM, which will enormously improve the detection sensitivity of fluorescence sensors. Compared with most fluorescent sensors, such as organic dyes, gold or silver nanoclusters, and semiconductor quantum dots, copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) are superior for the cost-effective and eco-friendly preparation without the use of noble metal salts, sophisticated ligands, organic solvents, and toxic reductant [50]. However, the sensing application of CuNCs is usually limited by their weak florescence and poor stability [50]. Notably, the entrapment of CuNCs into ZIF-8 is one of the most efficient strategies to simultaneously improve the fluorescent emission and stability of nanoclusters [35,36,51]. Therefore, it is desirable to explore the new path of CuNCs@ZIF-8 degradation for the broadened applications.

As a proof-of-concept, this work explores that HQ as a model is used to collapse ZIF-8 shell to leakage CuNCs through interacting with 2-MIM ( Scheme 1). Initially, the photoluminescence emission intensity of CuNCs is significantly improved after adding Zn2+ due to aggregation-induced emission (AIE). And then, CuNCs are inserted into ZIF-8 via in-situ growth synthesis approach, further enhancing its intensity and stability (Scheme 1A). As is well known, HQ is easily oxidized to p-benzoquinone by O2 in the air. The hydrogen bonding interaction between p-benzoquinone and 2-MIM causes the collapse of the ZIF-8 shell, resulting in the decline of the PL strength of CuNCs@ZIF-8 (Scheme 1B). So, the fluorescent method has been achieved for detection of HQ with a detection limit as low as 76 nM. Importantly, this method has good selectivity in seven phenolic analogues and two dihydroxybenzene isomers of HQ. Subsequently, since α-arbutin can release HQ catalyzed by α-Glu, the activity of α-Glu is detected with a detection limit down to 0.003 U/mL. Finally, the system has not only been applied to the determination of IC50 value for four kinds of inhibitors but also binary combined effect of three types of flavonoids inhibitors including baicalin, apigenin and silybin (Scheme 1C). Compared with the single use, reasonable combination of dietary flavonoids can show the antagonistic inhibitory effect of α-Glu to a certain extent.

Copper nitrate, zinc nitrate hexahydrate, sodium hydroxide, phenol, p-nitrophenol, p-aminophenol, and other phenolic substances were acquired from Beijing Chemical Plant (Beijing, China). 2-methylimidazole (2-MIM) and L-glutathione reducing agent (GSH) were purchased from Maclean (Shanghai, China). Both alanine (Ala) and cysteine (Cys) were purchased from Beijing Biodee Biotechnology Co., LTD. Catechol (CC), resorcinol (RC), hydroquinone (HQ), bovine Serum albumin (BSA), human serum albumin (HSA), Glucose oxidase (GOx), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), pyrophosphatase (PPase), lysozyme (Lys), trypsin (Try), α-arbutin, and α-Glu were obtained from Sigma Aldrich Reagents. All the chemicals used in the experiment reached reagent grade or above and were not further purified. The ultrapure water used in the experiment was purified by Millipore ultrapure water system in the United States.

The photoluminescence spectra and UV–vis spectra were obtained by Molecular Devices SpectraMax M2 multimode enzyme marker. X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) was obtained by D8 Advance diffractometer. The XPS maps were determined at Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB 250XI using a 300 W Xe lamp as a light source with an optical power density of 100 mW/cm2. Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed on the Nicolet 380 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were taken at 80 kV accelerated voltage with Hitachi H7650 instrument. The hydrodynamic diameters and Zeta potential of the sample were measured by using Malvern Zetasizer (UK).

CuNCs were produced with some changes from our earlier research [52]. Initially, GSH (5.0 mL, 40 mM) and copper nitrate hexahydrate (5.0 mL, 10 mM) were mixed and then stirred continuously for about 5 min to obtain the light-yellow solution. When the pH of the mixture is balanced to roughly 7.0 by using NaOH (1.0 M), the colorless and transparent CuNCs was synthesized.

In order to prepare CuNCs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites, Zn(NO3)2 (1.0 mL, 100 mM) was added into the synthesized GSH-CuNCs (2.0 mL) and agitated the mixture for about 5 min at room temperature. Next, 1.0 mL 2-MIM (365 mM) was introduced into the afore-mentioned mixture. The resulting CuNCs@ZIF-8 were obtained by reacting for roughly 10 min, and then centrifuging at 8000 rpm for about 5 min to collect them. The ultimate product was washed and centrifuged three times by using ultrapure water and methanol in turn, and then dried in a vacuum at 60 0C.

To determine HQ, 150 μL CuNCs@ZIF-8 (2.0 mg/mL) was mixed with 30 μL HQ (0–200 μM) aqueous solution. Then, 120 μL HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 7.4, 0.02 M) was added to adjust the total volume of the system to 300 μL and incubated the mixture at 25 0C for 30 min. After that, the PL spectra were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm and an emission wavelength of 600 nm. By adding 200 μL reaction solution into the 96-well plate. Each PL measurement was repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of HQ and its determination in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the selectivity of the system for HQ detection, the effects of HQ isomers (CC and RC) and their analogues (phenol, o-aminophenol, m-aminophenol, p-aminophenol, o-nitrophenol, p-nitrophenol, and phloroglucinol) were determined. The concentration of each interfering substance was the same as HQ at 60 μM.

To analyze the feasibility of the system for detecting HQ in real samples, the different concentrations of HQ was spiked into river water and tap water samples according to the steps of HQ determination, and the method was validated by conducting a standard addition recovery experiment.

To determine the activity of α-Glu, α-arbutin (30 μL, 1.0 mM), a 30 μL aliquot of α-Glu at various concentrations (0 ∼ 20 U/mL), and 90 μL HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 7.4, 0.02 M) were incubated at 37 0C for 30 min. After adding CuNCs@ZIF-8 (150 μL, 2.0 mg/mL), the mixture was kept at 25 0C for another 30 min and removed 200 μL reaction solution into the 96-well plate. At last, the emission spectra were collected at excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the system for detecting α-Glu, many interfering pieces including common metal ions (Na+, K+), amino acids (Ala, Cys), proteins (BSA, HSA), enzymes (ALP, PPase, Lys, GOx, Try, and α-Glu) were conducted both with and without α-Glu. The concentration of each metal ions, amino acids, protein, and enzyme was 1.0 mM, 1.0 mM, 0.05 mg /mL, 0.5 U /mL, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of α-Glu in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the activity of α-Glu in human serum, 100-fold dilutions of serum samples were prepared using HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 7.4, 0.02 M). 90 μL of diluted human serum samples were mixed with 30 μL of three different concentrations of -Glu (0.01, 0.1, and 0.5 U/mL). The resulting solution was then adjusted to a final volume of 300 μL. α-Glu was detected in human serum samples according to the procedure of α-Glu determination, and the method was verified by standard addition recovery experiment.

The screening of α-Glu inhibitors was conducted using the following experimental setup. Firstly, 30 μL of α-Glu inhibitors (acarbose, baicalein, silibinin, and apigenin) with different concentrations were mixed with 30 μL of α-Glu 5 U/mL. Next, 60 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 7.0, 0.02 M) was added and incubated at 37 0C for 15 min. Then 30 μL α-arbutin (1.0 mM) was introduced into the system and kept at 37 0C for another 30 min. Finally, 150 μL CuNCs@ZIF-8 (2.0 mg/mL) was added and the resultant solution was adequately incubated at 25 0C for another 30 min. Finally, 200 μL reaction solution was added into the 96-well plate, and emission spectra were recorded in a microplate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Combination effect test of α-Glu inhibitors
ce:section.ce:para: To study the effect of two combinations of three different dietary flavonoids (baicalein, silibinin, and apigenin) on α-Glu, a mixture of 30 μL two different flavonoid drugs was mixed with 30 μL of α-Glu at 5.0 U/mL. According to the steps for the determination of α-Glu inhibitors, combination effect of binary mixtures was measured.

A facile and efficient "bottle-around-ship" approach for preparing CuNCs@ ZIF-8 has been developed in three steps ( Fig. 1 A). Firstly, GSH is chosen as a dual-function reagent to serve as both a reducing agent and a stabilizer in the preparation of CuNCs by adjusting pH≈ 7.0 within 5 min. Secondly, CuNCs exhibit the AIE behavior after adding Zn2+. Finally, CuNCs are in-situ embedded into ZIF-8 during the construction of the framework to synthesize CuNCs@ZIF-8. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images show that CuNCs exhibit a spherical morphology with an average diameter of about 2.1 nm. The lattice spacing of approximately 0.20 nm corresponds to the (111) planes of the metallic Cu [53] (Fig. 1B). The TEM images indicate that the morphology of CuNCs@ZIF-8 possessing a remarkably uniform diameter of about 193 nm is rhombic dodecahedron (Fig. 1 C), which is consistent with shape of pure ZIF-8 (Fig. S2A). This evidence identifies that the normal morphology of the host ZIF-8 is barely impacted by the loaded CuNCs. The average size of CuNCs and CuNCs@ZIF-8 is estimated to be 4.3 nm and 252 nm by dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements (Fig. 1D), respectively. FTIR spectra of CuNCs and CuNCs@ZIF-8 show that the peak at 2525 cm−1 does not appear [54], which is attributed to the stretching vibration mode of S-H groups in GSH molecules (Fig. 1E). This data validates that GSH forms covalent bonds with the Cu core through Cu-S connections. In particular, the signal associated with Zn-N stretching in CuNCs@ZIF-8 nanocomposite appears at 421 cm−1, demonstrating excellent agreement with the corresponding signal of pure ZIF-8 [55]. CuNCs@ZIF-8 exhibits an X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern at 2 of 14.5, 28.0, 38.5, and 49.60, which is well matched to pure ZIF-8 phase (Fig. 1 F), demonstrating that adding CuNCs had little impact on the ZIF-8's crystal structure. Moreover, the XRD pattern of the CuNCs@ZIF-8 composite does not exhibit distinct peaks corresponding to CuNCs. This suggests that the CuNCs are aggregated within the pores of the ZIF-8 rather than being physically adsorbed onto the surface of the ZIF-8 [56]. Moreover, the consistent distribution of Zn, Cu, N, and S elements in CuNCs@ZIF-8 can be seen in the energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) mapping images, confirming that CuNCs are homogeneously incorporated into ZIF-8 (Fig. 1 G). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis confirms the presence of S, C, N, O, and Cu elements in CuNCs@ZIF-8 (Fig. 1H). Moreover, the Cu 2p XPS peaks of CuNCs@ZIF-8 exhibit two distinct peaks at 953.2 and 932.7 eV, which corresponds to the 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 electrons of Cu(0) and Cu(I), respectively. Notably, the peak at 943 eV is not observed, revealing that Cu(II) is absent in this system (Fig. 1I). Moreover, the characteristic peak of CuNCs@ZIF-8 obviously blue-shifts from 620 to 600 nm and while almost no shift of excitation wavelength is observed compared to CuNCs (Fig. 1 J). These results manifest that CuNCs are well-inserted in the ZIF-8 framework.

It is evident that the fluorescence emission CuNCs at 600 nm improved dramatically after being encapsulated into ZIF-8 shell with the excitation at 340 nm ( Fig. 2 A, red curve), which is nearly 120 times more than that of CuNCs. Correspondingly, CuNCs@ZIF-8 exhibits intense orange-red fluorescence emission under a 365 nm UV lamp (Inset of Fig. 2 A). Interestingly, the strong fluorescence of CuNCs@ZIF-8 is conspicuously quenched after introducing HQ (Fig. 2 A, blue curve). As can been seen from TEM images in Fig. 2B, the structure of CuNCs@ZIF-8 shell is damaged after adding HQ, and then some CuNCs are released from ZIF-8, leading to fluorescence quenching. The following DLS measurement ascertains that the average hydrodynamic size of CuNCs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites with about 91 nm after adding HQ is much smaller than that before adding HQ with approximately 256 nm (Fig. 2 C). As displayed in Fig. 2D, the fluorescence lifetime of CuNCs@ZIF-8 with HQ (4.77 μs) is distinctly shorter than that without HQ (15.62 μs), which is close with CuNCs (3.86 μs). The decreasing lifetime strengthens the indirect evidence for the collapse of CuNCs@ZIF-8 shell. Coating efficiency of CuNCs within ZIF-8 was calculated to be about 94.6% in our previous report [57].

Besides, the excitation spectra of CuNCs@ZIF-8 and the absorption spectrum of HQ had essentially no overlap, ruling out the IFE as a potential sensing mechanism for CuNCs@ZIF-8 fluorescence quenching (Fig. 2E). It is noteworthy that a new absorption peak appears at 415 nm after mixing HQ with 2-MIM, implying the interaction between HQ and 2-MIM (Fig. 2 F). While Zn2+ have little effect on HQ in views of their absorption spectra (Fig. S1). FTIR spectrum is also used to study the interaction between HQ and 2-MIM. For HQ, the band at 1518 cm−1 is corresponding to the C-O vibration stretching vibration and the absorption peaks at 3200–3600 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups (Fig. 2 G, green curve). Nevertheless, in the FT-IR spectra of the HQ+ 2-MIM mixture, the distinctive peak at 1518 cm−1 disappeared (Fig. 2 G, red curve). Moreover, a new peak observed at 1660 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of the carbonyl groups for the mixture of HQ and 2-MIM, suggesting a possible conversion of HQ to p-benzoquinone. Furthermore, the addition of HQ causes a reduction in the zeta potential of CuNCs@ZIF-8, from + 16.8 mV to + 7.52 mV (Fig. 2I). The above results suggest that p-benzoquinone not only interacts with 2-MIM but also bond to Zn2+, which is possible responsible for degradation of CuNCs@ZIF-8 shell.

To further demonstrate the quenching effect of p-benzoquinone on CuNCs@ZIF-8 rather than HQ, GSH is introduced into the HQ on CuNCs@ZIF-8 system since GSH is widely recognized as a reducing reagent for p-benzoquinone [11]. It is evident that GSH alone has little impact on CuNCs@ZIF-8′s fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2H, blue curve). However, when GSH is introducing to the CuNCs@ZIF-8 +HQ system, the fluorescence of CuNCs@ZIF-8 is completely restored (Fig. 2H, red curve), demonstrating that the degradation of CuNCs@ZIF-8 is primarily caused by p-benzoquinone rather than HQ. Surprisingly, adding HQ has no discernible effects on the shape or average size of pure ZIF-8, indicating that ZIF-8 cannot be decomposed (Fig. S2). While CuNCs@ZIF-8 shell can be destroyed after adding HQ according to TEM images and hydrodynamic diameter of particles (Fig. 2B and C). The fluorescence intensity of CuNCs shows little variation after adding HQ, 2-MIM, the mixture of HQ and 2-MIM alone (Fig. S3A). Noteworthy is the fact that the AIE behavior of CuNCs is induced by Zn2+ owing to the coordination of ligand carboxyl groups and Zn2+ (Fig. S3B and C), which possibly weakening its coordination with 2-MIM in ZIF-8 framework. Overall, these results confirm that the fluorescence quenching of CuNCs@ZIF-8 is ascribe to the decomposition of the ZIF-8 shell owing to the hydrogen bonding interaction between p-benzoquinone and 2-MIM.

An easy fluorescent approach has been proposed for HQ detection based on the collapse of CuNCs@ZIF-8 shell ( Fig. 3 A). To guarantee the sensitivity and reliability of this fluorescence method, some optimized parameters were chosen as follows: CuNCs@ZIF-8 at 1.0 mg/mL, reaction time for 30 min, temperature at 25 0C, and pH 7.0 for the following experiments (Fig. S4). Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence emission spectra of CuNCs@ZIF-8 were investigated. Noticeably, with an increase in HQ concentration, CuNCs@ZIF-8′s intensity at 600 nm decreases (Fig. 3B). Within the range of 0.1 to 50 mΜ, a remarkable linear relationship exists between the concentration of HQ and the fluorescence intensity (F) (Fig. 3 C). The linear regression equation is expressed as F = −41 CHQ + 2169 with correlation ecoefficiency of R2 = 0.996 (Fig. 3D) and the LOD is calculate to be 76 nM according to 3δ/S rule (δ represents the standard deviation of the blank signal, while S denotes the slope of the linear calibration plot). This value is lower than the standard of the U.S. Environment Protection Agency, which estimates that the maximum wastewater discharge is 0.5 mg/L (∼4.5 µM) [2,11]. Additionally, the LOD is lower than or equivalent to several published fluorescent nanomaterials-based techniques (Table S1), indicating that our detecting system had superior analytical capabilities.

Before being utilized for HQ detection in real samples, the selectivity of our assay is investigated in the presence of additional possible interference substances, particularly certain isomers that share the same chemical formula, such as CC, and RC. It is noted that these interfering substances show negligible or slightly effect on the fluorescence intensity of CuNCs@ZIF-8 except for HQ. More importantly, the strong fluorescence of CuNCs@ZIF-8 is significantly quenched upon addition of HQ, while two isomers of HQ (CC and RC) do not have influence on the fluorescence intensity, size distribution of DLS hydrodynamic diameter, and morphology of CuNCs@ZIF-8 (Fig. 3E, Fig. S5, and Fig. S6). The remarkable absorbance of the mixture of HQ and 2-MIM at 415 nm is obtained (Fig. 3 F), suggesting that HQ is converted into the corresponding p-benzoquinones [58]. Simultaneously their solution immediately becomes a deep orange color (inset of Fig. 3 F). While CC or RC solution is still colorless after adding 2-MIM. Notably, the pH ≈ 9.0 value of the alkaline 2-MIM activates the oxidation of HQ. It was further supported that HQ is easily oxidized at pH ≈ 9.0 with NaOH and the solution shows also a dark orange color (Fig. S7). These data demonstrate that HQ is more easily oxidized to p-benzoquinones in alkaline condition than CC and RC. Additionally, the interference reagent of S2- has almost little influence on the fluorescence intensity of CuNCs@ZIF-8, which is not consistent with the previous report based on carbon dots@ZIF-8 [59]. As a result, this method had a good selectivity toward HQ.

To evaluate the reliability and applicability of our fluorescence assay, the concentration of HQ in real samples was tested. Table 1 shows the results from water samples. No HQ content is detected in the water samples initially. However, when tap water and lake water samples are spiked with various concentrations of HQ, the assay exhibits excellent performance with recoveries ranging from 97.43% to 102.40% and relative standard deviations (RSD) of no more than 2.51% (n = 3). These findings indicate the potential of our assay for accurately detecting HQ levels in real matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination for α-Glu activity and screening its inhibitors

ce:section.ce:para: We further explored the feasibility for α-Glu activity detection. The sensing principle of the fluorescent assay for α-Glu activity and its inhibitors screening is illustrated in Fig. 4 A. Fortunately, after the introduction of α-Glu or α-arbutin into the CuNCs@ZIF-8 system individually, the fluorescence intensity has no distinguishable change in respective to control solution (Fig. 4B). It unveils that neither α-Glu nor α-arbutin can directly react with CuNCs@ZIF-8. In striking contrast, the fluorescence peak at 600 nm is remarkably quenching when both α-Glu and substrate are added together. At the same time, the average hydrodynamic size of CuNCs@ZIF-8 is estimated to be about 97.5 nm in the presence of both α-Glu and α-arbutin, which is similar with that after adding HQ, but much smaller than pure CuNCs@ZIF-8 approximately 255.6 nm by DLS analysis (Fig. 4 C). Moreover, the TEM image testifies the collapse of CuNCs@ZIF-8 framework after adding α-Glu and α-arbutin (inset of Fig. 4 C). These results confirm the feasibility of using the fluorescence method to detect α-Glu activity. Therefore, α-arbutin can be catalytically degraded by α-Glu as a substrate to release HQ. Thus, α-Glu activity can be detected rationally based on CuNCs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites.

To ensure that the determination of α-Glu activity is accurate and sensitive, some crucial factors, such as incubation time, α-arbutin concentration, pH, and reaction temperature are optimized. As the incubation time and the concentration of α-Glu increase, the fluorescence intensity decreases first and then stabilize after 30 min (Fig. S8A). The intensity gradually decreases with the increasing concentration of α-arbutin and no longer fluctuates when α-arbutin beyond a concentration of 100 μM (Fig. S8B). The lowest intensity is acquired at pH 7.0 (Fig. S8C). Reaction temperature at 37 0C gives the lowest intensity (Fig. S8D). So, the ideal conditions are as follows: reaction time for 30 min, concentration of α-arbutin at 100 μM, pH = 7.0, and reaction temperature 37 0C.

The detection of α-Glu activity was carried out under optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence intensity of CuNCs@ZIF-8 gradually declines with increasing the concentration of α-Glu when α-arbutin existed. The superior linear relationship between the intensity and α-Glu activity in the range of 0.005–0.500 U/mL (Fig. 4E). The regression equation is F= −3420 Cα-Glu+ 2204 with the correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.991 and LOD is estimated to be 0.003 U/mL (3σ/k, n = 3), which is similar or lower than that of other previous reporters (Table S2).

To investigate the glucosidase activity selectivity of this assay, some possible interfering species including common metal ions (Na+ and K+), amino acids (Ala and Cys), proteins (BSA and HSA), and enzymes (ALP, PPas, Lys, Try, GOx, and Try) were tested. As depicted in Fig. 4 F, when α-Glu existed alone or coexists with interfering substances, the intensity of CuNCs@ZIF-8 is dramatically decreased in the presence of α-arbutin, while very negligible variations are achieved upon adding other interference because they cannot catalyze the hydrolysis of α-arbutin to 2-HQ. In additional, the interference reagent of butyrylcholinesterase (BChE) has almost little influence on the fluorescence intensity of CuNCs@ZIF-8, which is not consistent with the previous literature based on carbon dots@ZIF-8 [60]. These results imply that this approach has strong anti-interference ability for α-Glu assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: α-Glu assay in human serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: The potential feasibility of our proposed approach for detecting α-Glu activity is verified in human serum samples containing various proteins and other molecules. Three human serum samples that have been diluted 333-fold and spiked with α-Glu concentrations of 10.00, 100.00, and 500.00 mU/mL are measured, respectively. As depicted in Table 2, the satisfactory recoveries between 92.40% and 105.00% with RSD of lower than 4.37% (n = 3) are gained, suggesting the excellent accuracy and reliability of the sensing system for detecting α-Glu in complicated biological matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of a single inhibitor of α-Glu activity
ce:section.ce:para:
As is widely known, it is of great importance to design convenient techniques for screening novel and effective inhibitors of α-Glu for the therapy of diabetes mellitus. Acarbose, a commonly anti-diabetic drug, is firstly taken as the example to certify the screening inhibitor capacity of α-Glu by our assay. As illustrated in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence quenching of CuNCs@ZIF-8 is progressively recovered with the increasing dosage of acarbose because the α-Glu activity is precisely suppressed. The inhibition efficiency (IE) of the inhibitor is expressed by the following equation:
(1) IE (%) = [(F – F′)/(F0 -F′)] ×100

Where F0, F′, and F represents the fluorescence intensity of CuNCs@ZIF-8, CuNCs@ZIF-8 + α-arbutin+ α-Glu, and CuNCs@ZIF-8 + α-arbutin+ α-Glu+inhibitor, respectively. A sigmoid function curve can be observed by correlating the IE value with acarbose concentration in Fig. 5B. The inhibitor concentration needed to reduce enzyme activity by 50% is indicated by the IC50 value. The IC50 value of acarbose is approximately 37 nM, indicating the outstanding inhibitory effect, which is comparable or lower with other previous reports [10,19,24,30]. The constructed screening platform is utilized to assess the inhibition efficiency of three flavonoid inhibitors including baicalein, apigenin, and silibinin. Generally, an inhibitor's stronger inhibitory activity is often indicated by a lower IC50 value. As displayed in Fig. 5 C, baicalein possesses the lowest IC50 value of 155 nМ, suggesting to the highest inhibitory activity. IC50 value of silibinin and apigenin is up to 1940 nM and 2930 nM (Fig. 5D and E), respectively. The relative inhibitory activity order of the three flavonoids is as follows: baicalein>silibinin>apigenin (Fig. 5 F), which agrees with the earlier reports [19,22,30]. This novel platform based on CuNCs@ZIF-8 + α-arbutin offers a sensitive and practical approach for both screening α-Glu inhibitors and assessing their inhibition efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of synergy inhibition of α-Glu activity
ce:section.ce:para:
In some illness treatments, the combination usage of two medications to strengthen the inhibitory effect is a more effective technique than just raising the dose of a single agent. The combined effect is evaluated using the combination index (CI), which is a quantitative parameter. The CI value of less than 0.9 indicates the higher inhibitory effect against α-Glu than that of a drug used alone, which is defines synergism. The CI value in the range from 0.9 to 1.1 represents the equivalent inhibitory effect, which is denoted an additive effect. The CI value of more than 1.1 means the less inhibitory effect, which is described antagonism. The CI value is expressed by the following equation:
(2) CI = D1/Cx1 + D2 /Cx2

where D1 and D2 represent the doses of the first inhibitor and the second inhibitor in the combination system at a certain IE value, while Cx1 and Cx2 represent the doses of the first inhibitor and second inhibitor added alone at the same IE value. Fig. 6A shows that using baicalein and apigenin together significantly increases the suppression of α-Glu activity compared to using either compound alone. Noticeably, the CI values for the mixture of baicalein and apigenin are less than 0.9, suggesting that the combination use of these two drugs displays synergism. Baicalein and silibinin together have CI values that are less than 0.9 at lower doses and more than 1.1 at the highest concentration, indicating antagonism (Fig. 6B). The combination use of apigenin and silibinin is also similar phenomenon (Fig. 6C). Therefore, a strategic flavonoid combination can improve the effectiveness of α-Glu activity's inhibition, which would effectively reduce postprandial hyperglycemia and alleviate diabetes. These findings make it abundantly evident that this innovative method can be applied to the screening of α-Glu inhibitors and evaluating synergistic effect of the combining drugs in drug discovery.

In conclusion, a novel strategy has been rational designed for the collapse of CuNCs@ZIF-8 framework, which is triggered by p-benzoquinone through the hydrogen bonding interaction between p-benzoquinone and 2-MIM. Amazingly, the higher reducibility of HQ compared to other phenols including isomers, a highly specific and sensitive fluorescence HQ test is configured based on CuNCs@ZIF-8. Further, promoted by the conversion of 4-arbutin into HQ catalyzed by α-Glu, CuNCs@ZIF-8 has cleverly offered as a sensitive fluorescence technique for measuring α-Glu activity. The detection limit reaches 0.003 U/mL. The method has effectively demonstrated its capability for ultrasensitive detection of α-Glu in human serum samples, as well as screening various α-Glu inhibitors. This study not only opens new avenues for the practical use of based-ZIF-8 materials, but it also plays an underappreciated role in advancing the development of novel HQ-based α-Glu tests.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Wei Huang is doing her MS work at Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University. She is working toward sensors.

Yueying Liu received her MS degree and Ph.D degree from Beijing Institute of Technology. After two years of postdoctoral research at Tsinghua University, she began her own independent career in Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University. Her research interest is focus on the development of optical sensors.
Nan Yao is doing her Bachelor work at Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University. She is working toward nanomaterials.
Xiwen Zhang is doing her MS work at Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University. She is working toward biosensors.
Nan Wang is doing her MS work at Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University. She is currently working toward nanomaterials.

Yuexiang Lu received his Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Tsinghua University. He is currently working at institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology, Tsinghua University. His research interest is nanomaterial.
